filter_applied: Strict Malta-relevance filter: all items have direct connection to Malta as victim, attacker, regulator or legal jurisdiction

items.title: NEW — Malta Police Warn of Surge in Spoofed Calls Impersonating Officers
items.summary: The Malta Police Force issued an urgent public warning about a surge in fraudulent phone calls where scammers impersonate police officers using spoofed local mobile numbers. Callers claim to be police officials, ask questions, then request personal details to schedule a fictitious appointment. Police warned that scammers increasingly use AI and deepfake videos — including one featuring Prime Minister Robert Abela — to target victims. Superintendent Jonathan Ransley stated 'everyone is a potential victim, not because people are ignorant, but because scams are getting more sophisticated.'
items.malta_connection: Malta Police Force issued the warning; Maltese citizens are the targets; deepfake of Maltese PM Robert Abela used as lure

items.title: NEW — Malta Police Arrest Woman for €1M Fraud Scam Targeting 200 Victims
items.summary: Malta Police arrested a 25-year-old woman and an 82-year-old man in connection with a fraud investigation linked to losses of approximately €1 million affecting around 200 victims. The case was investigated by the Financial Crime Investigations Department (FCID). This arrest follows a broader pattern of escalating financial cybercrime in Malta, with the FCID handling over 3,700 fraud investigations between 2022 and 2025.
items.malta_connection: Suspects arrested by Malta Police; victims are Maltese residents; FCID (Malta's financial crime unit) led the investigation

items.title: MGA Breach: Wittmann Used LinkedIn and X to Claim Responsibility — LinkedIn Post Rapidly Removed
items.source: AGB Brief · Newsbook · Next.io · iGaming Expert
items.summary: Lilith Wittmann posted on both LinkedIn and X claiming responsibility for the MGA breach — the LinkedIn post spread rapidly across the iGaming industry before being removed by the platform. She warned: 'Any police action from Malta would trigger the immediate release of my entire archive of iGaming-related data.' The MGA condemned the unauthorised access but confined its public response to IT incident language, conspicuously silent on the substance of Wittmann's organised crime allegations. Three simultaneous processes are now underway: Malta's criminal investigation, Germany's awareness of Wittmann's public statements, and Wittmann's possession of a data archive she is using as both a calling card and a bargaining chip.
items.malta_connection: MGA is Malta's primary gaming regulator; Wittmann held access for months; data shared with media and Maltese authorities; criminal investigation opened in Malta

items.title: Nationalist Party (PN) Ransomware Attack — 21,000 People's Data Dumped on Dark Web
items.summary: Cybercriminals hacked the Nationalist Party's servers, demanded €5,000 ransom not to leak data, and when the PN refused to negotiate, dumped personal information of over 21,000 people on the dark web. The PN believed the exposed data related to records from 2014 or earlier. Files were published to the hackers' forum on the dark web. The attack highlighted the vulnerability of Maltese political organisations to ransomware.
items.malta_connection: Nationalist Party is a Maltese political party; 21,000 individuals whose data was exposed are Maltese citizens; data dumped publicly affecting Maltese people

items.title: Evos Oil Terminal Birzebbuga Hit by BlackCat Ransomware in European-Wide Attack
items.summary: The Evos oil storage terminal in Birzebbuga, Malta suffered IT disruptions as part of a coordinated ransomware wave hitting 17 European oil and fuel terminals. Evos confirmed 'disruption of IT services' at its Malta facility causing 'delays in execution.' The attack was attributed to the BlackCat/ALPHV ransomware group and simultaneously hit Oiltanking and Mabanaft in Germany and SEA-Invest in Belgium. Malta's critical energy infrastructure was directly impacted.
items.malta_connection: Evos operates Malta's Birzebbuga oil storage terminal — part of Malta's critical energy infrastructure — directly confirmed as impacted

items.title: MFSA Issues 'Heightened Cyber Threat' Circular to All Supervised Financial Entities
items.summary: The MFSA issued a circular titled 'Exercising Caution in Times of Heightened Cyber Threat' on 5 March 2026, warning all MFSA-supervised entities of an elevated cyber risk environment linked to global conflicts. The circular follows MFSA's 2026 Supervisory Priorities placing ICT and cyber-resilience under DORA as a top concern. Entities were urged to conduct situational awareness assessments and immediately revisit ICT risk management procedures.
items.malta_connection: MFSA is Malta's financial regulator; circular issued to all Malta-licensed financial entities

items.title: Multi-Bank Phishing Scheme: 100+ Victims Across BOV, APS, HSBC and BNF — Suspect Denied Bail
items.summary: A coordinated phishing operation using fake bank apps and SMS links mimicking BOV's login portal defrauded 100+ victims across four Maltese banks between September 2024 and May 2025. A 25-year-old suspect was arrested in February 2026 and denied bail, with investigators detecting international involvement and cryptocurrency money laundering via Revolut and Bitcoin.
items.malta_connection: Four Malta-licensed banks targeted; all victims are Maltese bank customers; suspect arrested by Malta Police

items.title: BOV Fraud Losses Nearly Doubled — €5.4M in 8 Months of 2025; 'Spot the Scam' Tool Launched
items.summary: Bank of Valletta revealed fraud losses nearly doubled from €2.9M in 2024 to €5.4M in the first eight months of 2025, with the FCID simultaneously probing €13M in total scam losses in H1 2025. BOV launched a free 'Spot the Scam Self-Assessment' tool in April 2026 for Maltese consumers. Separately, 28.7% of Maltese enterprises reported a cyber incident in 2023 — well above the EU average of 21.5% (Eurostat 2024).
items.malta_connection: BOV is Malta's largest bank; all fraud losses involve Maltese customers; FCID is Malta's financial crime police unit

items.title: Fast Track CRM Cyberattack Exposes Casino Player KYC Documents Including Passports
items.summary: Malta-headquartered iGaming CRM provider Fast Track was breached, exposing player KYC documents including passports and driver's licences across at least two casino operators. Shuffle Casino was the only publicly named victim and terminated its partnership. The breach occurred despite Fast Track holding a SOC 2 Type 2 certification renewed just months earlier in June 2025.
items.malta_connection: Fast Track is headquartered in Malta and holds an MGA licence; breach reported to Maltese authorities

items.title: Saint James Hospital — Malta's First Confirmed Healthcare Cyberattack
items.summary: Saint James Hospital Group confirmed a sophisticated cyberattack on 18 April 2025, immediately isolating all systems from external networks. A forensic investigation was launched into potential patient data exposure. Both the IDPC and Malta Police were formally notified — the first confirmed cyberattack on Maltese healthcare infrastructure, consistent with an EU-wide trend of hospitals being targeted.
items.malta_connection: Saint James Hospital operates in Sliema and Zejtun, Malta; patients are Maltese residents; IDPC and Malta Police formally notified
